info.parenttitle: A3.3 Introduction to mechanical systems (HL only)

markdown:
## Gear Systems


<definition term="Gear">A **toothed wheel** that **meshes** with another to **transmit motion** or **change speed/direction**.</definition>

#### Key Components of Gear Systems


1. **Gear Teeth**: **Interlocking projections** that **engage** with **other gears**.
2. **Gear Shaft**: The **axis** around which the **gear rotates**.
3. **Pitch Circle**: The **imaginary circle** where **gear teeth** **engage **which determines gear size and spacing.
4. **Module**: The **ratio** of the **pitch diameter** to the **number of teeth**.
5. **Pressure Angle**: The angle between the gear tooth profile and the gear tangent: affects smoothness and strength of transmission.

![Parts of a basic gear mechanism (instructables)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/e668d2a183063f21dfc51f70cedd4c3728283b29-270x225.png)

<callout type="note">The **module **is a **critical parameter **in **gear design**, ensuring **compatibility **between **gears**.

* A **high gear ratio** (e.g. 3:1) = increased torque, reduced speed.
* A **low gear ratio** (e.g. 1:3) = increased speed, reduced torque.</callout>

### Types of Gear Systems


#### Spur Gears


<definition term="Spur Gear">A toothed gear with **teeth that project parallel to the gear’s axis**, used to transmit motion between **parallel shafts**.</definition>

1. **Use**: **Transmit motion** between **parallel shafts**.
2. **Example**: **Clocks**, **conveyor belts**.

![Laser cut wooden clock that uses a spur gear (eliaslundgaard)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5924f12045b63c109a7be68a211baa29e87a1e4d-1280x720.png)

<callout type="note">Spur gears are the most common type of gear due to their **simplicity** and **efficiency**.</callout>

#### Bevel Gears


<definition term="Bevel Gear">A pair of **toothed rotating gears** that transfer power between **intersecting shafts**, usually at a **90° angle**.</definition>

1. **Use**: Transmit motion between intersecting shafts (usually at 90 degrees).
2. **Example**: Differentials in automobiles.

![Bevel Gear (welleshaft)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0c7b2afe62e7167912b7778c45e6bd29b79b36cd-1024x780.png)

<callout type="note">Bevel gears are essential for **changing the direction of motion** in machinery.</callout>

#### Rack and Pinion


<definition term="Rack & Pinion">A mechanical system where a **toothed bar (rack)** engages with a **circular gear (pinion)** to **convert rotary motion into linear motion**.</definition>

1. **Use**: Convert rotary motion to linear motion.
2. **Example**: Steering systems in cars.

![Rack & Pinion with servo geared motor (stober)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/210360303a30fb7048b0757157927de6e5ae647b-2126x1417.png)

<inlineVideo caption="Rack & Pinion used in car steering" />

<callout type="note">Rack and pinion systems are ideal for applications requiring **precise linear movement**.</callout>

#### Worm Gears


1. **Use**: Transmit motion at right angles with high reduction ratios.
2. **Example**: Elevator systems, tuning mechanisms in musical instruments.

![Worm gear (baer)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/43b136db8697a2a5ee44a4e31b00d8d68c8af02d-302x167.png)

<callout type="note">Worm gears provide **high torque reduction** but are **less efficient** due to sliding friction.</callout>

#### Ratchet and Pawl


<definition term="Ratchet and Pawl">A mechanical device that allows **motion in one direction only**. The **pawl fits into the teeth** of the **ratchet wheel**, preventing backward rotation while allowing forward movement.</definition>

1. **Use**: Allow motion in one direction only.
2. **Example**: Wrenches, bicycle freewheels.

![Ratchet and pawl ](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/848705d5c9630c72925408093c2c1f9161ca92d8-1500x1500.png)

![A wrench using a ratchet and pawl gear system](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/04d7eb9ad6c6a62715d89586406705ccc9f8333a-1536x1024.png)

<callout type="note">Ratchet and pawl systems are crucial for **preventing backsliding** in mechanical systems.</callout>

#### Idler Gears


1. **Use**: Reverse the direction of rotation or maintain a specific distance between gears.
2. **Example**: Conveyor systems.

![Idler gear used in conveyor belts (gram conveyor)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3ae12f01e372bbf57cdbecb73fe564662fee542e-1200x675.png)

<callout type="note">* **Idler** gears are often misunderstood as changing gear ratios. 
* Their primary function is to **alter the direction of rotation** or **maintain spacing**.</callout>

#### Compound Gears


1. **Use**: Achieve high gear ratios in compact spaces.
2. **Example**: Gearboxes in vehicles.

![Compound gears (glydeck)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/96f3b4ce31cae9c0afc69bf294118af6800b3b9e-512x175.png)

<callout type="analogy">Compound gears are essential for applications requiring significant speed reduction or torque multiplication.</callout>

### How Gear Systems Work


1. **Engagement**: Teeth of one gear mesh with another, transmitting motion.
2. **Torque and Speed**: Larger gears reduce speed but increase torque, smaller gears do the opposite.
3. **Direction**: Gears can change the direction of rotation.

<callout type="note">* **Gear Ratios - **The **gear ratio** is the ratio of the number of teeth on two interlocking gears. 
* It determines the **relationship between speed and torque**.

$$\text{Gear Ratio} = \frac{\text{Number of Teeth on Driven Gear}}{\text{Number of Teeth on Driver Gear}}$$</callout>

<callout type="tip">When analyzing a gear system, always consider the gear ratio, as it determines the relationship between **input** and **output speed** and **torque**.

* **Smaller gear** driving a **larger gear**: Reduces speed but increases torque.
* **Larger gear **driving a **smaller gear**: Increases speed but reduces torque.</callout>

<callout type="self_review">Identify three types of gear systems and explain their specific applications in real-world scenarios.</callout>

###
